topic: thinking about teaching Deniz the straight razor

turns.content: I have a question... not about technology or anything like that. more of a... how do you say... a teaching question. about timing. when is the right time to teach someone something dangerous

That's a thoughtful question. It depends a lot on the context — what are you thinking about teaching, and to whom? "Dangerous" could mean a lot of things, and the answer changes depending on the skill, the student, and what's at stake.

the straight razor. I am a barber... and I have an apprentice, Deniz. he has been learning well, the basics, the comb work, the scissors. but the straight razor... this is different. this is a blade on a man's throat. there is no room for a mistake. and I keep going back and forth... is he ready, is he not ready

That's a real responsibility — both for you as the teacher and for Deniz as the student. A straight razor is one of those skills where the learning curve has real consequences. It's not like a bad haircut that grows back.

A few things to consider:

1. **Has he mastered the prerequisites?** — Steady hands, good posture, understanding of angles. The fundamentals of blade work with scissors often translate.
2. **Does he show patience, not just skill?** — A rushed barber with a straight razor is dangerous. Does Deniz take his time, or does he still try to go fast?
3. **Does he respect the tool?** — Not fear it, but respect it. There's a difference. Fear makes your hand shake. Respect makes your hand careful.

What's your gut telling you? You've been watching him every day.

my gut says... almost. almost but not yet. he is still... how to say this... he moves like a young man. quick, confident, sometimes too confident. when he holds the scissors he is good, yes. but I watch his hands and they are not quiet yet. you understand? the hands need to be quiet before you put a blade on skin

"The hands need to be quiet" — that's a perfect way to describe it. You're looking for a kind of stillness, a control that comes from the inside, not just muscle memory. And you're right that confidence without calm is dangerous with a blade.

It sounds like you can see the potential but you're waiting for a shift — from youthful energy to disciplined precision. That shift usually doesn't happen on a schedule. It happens when the student stops trying to impress and starts trying to be precise.

Have you talked to him about it? Does he know the straight razor is coming, or are you watching quietly and waiting for the right moment to introduce it?

oh he knows... he asks me every week. 'usta, when will you teach me the razor?' every week, like clockwork. and I say the same thing every time... 'when you are ready.' and he rolls his eyes like all young people do. but that is the point, you see... if he understood why I am making him wait, he would already be ready

That's actually profound. The impatience itself is the proof he's not ready yet. The day he stops asking — the day he just comes in, does his work quietly, and trusts the process — that's probably the day you hand him the razor.

It reminds me of the old idea that the student who says "I'm ready" usually isn't, and the student who says "I still have more to learn" usually is.

You clearly have a philosophy about this. How were you taught? Did your own teacher make you wait?

yes... a long time. I swept floors for months before I touched a comb. and the razor... maybe a year, maybe more. I cannot remember exactly. but I remember the feeling of wanting it so badly and being told to wait. and now I am on the other side... I am the one saying wait. life is funny like this, you know. you become the person you were frustrated with

That's such an honest observation — becoming the person who frustrated you, and finally understanding why they did it. That's the cycle of real mentorship. Your teacher wasn't being difficult. He was protecting you, and protecting the customers, and protecting the craft. And now you're doing the same.

The fact that you went through it yourself means you know exactly what to look for in Deniz. You'll recognize the readiness when it comes because you remember it in yourself — that moment when the wanting turned into something calmer and steadier.

Do you have a plan for how to start him? Practicing on a balloon, on a watermelon, or going straight to skin with supervision?

I was thinking... first the strop. teach him to sharpen, to care for the blade. a month of just that. if he cannot respect the tool he cannot use the tool. then maybe... practice on his own face. you learn fastest when it is your own skin. and only then, a real customer, with me standing right behind him... hand on his shoulder if needed

That's an excellent progression. Each step builds something specific:

1. **Stropping and maintenance** — teaches him the blade is alive, it needs attention, it's not just a tool you pick up and put down. A month of this builds reverence.
2. **His own face** — this is the best teacher. When you feel the blade against your own skin, you learn pressure, angle, and consequence all at once. No balloon or watermelon can teach that.
3. **First customer, with you right there** — the hand on the shoulder is everything. He has the blade, but you have the control. He'll feel your presence and it will steady him.

This is the kind of structured teaching that most people don't have the patience for anymore. You're not just teaching him a skill — you're shaping how he thinks about the work.

that is exactly it... it is not about the razor. the razor is just metal. it is about the man holding it. what kind of barber will he become... careful or careless, patient or rushed. this is what I am really teaching. we say 'ağaç yaşken eğilir'... the tree bends while it is young. and Deniz is still young, still bending. I must bend him in the right direction

The tree bends while it is young — that's the perfect proverb for this moment. You're shaping something that will set for a lifetime. The habits he builds now, the discipline, the respect for the craft — that's what he'll carry into every shave he ever gives, long after he leaves your chair.

Here's a thought — when you do start the razor lessons, you might also want to talk to him about why it matters. Not just the technique, but the philosophy. The reason the customer trusts you with a blade at their throat. That understanding is what separates a craftsman from someone who just knows the motions.

yes... the trust. this is the word. when a man tilts his head back and closes his eyes and you hold the blade to his neck... he is giving you something. his trust, his vulnerability. and you must honor that. every single time. this is what I will teach Deniz... not just where to hold the blade, but what it means that someone lets you hold it there. danke for listening to me think through this, my friend. I feel clearer now

That's the heart of it right there — the trust and what it means to honor it. When you teach Deniz that, you're not just making a barber. You're making someone worthy of the chair.

Glad I could help you think it through. You'll know when it's time, Mehmet. You've been watching hands your whole life. Trust that. And when the day comes, that first razor lesson will be one of those moments neither of you forgets. Take your time with it.
